Implement a self-contained HTML function that dynamically generates a keyboard-navigable, accessible dropdown menu. The dropdown should include at least five options, and it should be accessible using ARIA attributes. The function should be able to be called multiple times on the same page without conflicting IDs. Use JavaScript to handle the dropdown behavior, including opening and closing the menu when the user presses the arrow keys or clicks on the dropdown button. Ensure that the first option in the dropdown is focused when the menu is opened. Include appropriate ARIA roles and properties to ensure accessibility.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Accessible Dropdown</title>
    <style>
        .dropdown {
            position: relative;
            display: inline-block;
        }
        .dropdown-content {
            display: none;
            position: absolute;
            background-color: #f9f9f9;
            min-width: 160px;
            box-shadow: 0px 8px 16px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.2);
            z-index: 1;
        }
        .dropdown-content button {
            color: black;
            padding: 12px 16px;
            text-decoration: none;
            display: block;
            width: 100%;
            text-align: left;
        }
        .dropdown-content button:focus {
            outline: none;
            background-color: #ddd;
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>

<script>
function createAccessibleDropdown(id, options) {
    const dropdown = document.createElement('div');
    dropdown.className = 'dropdown';
    dropdown.setAttribute('id', `dropdown-${id}`);

    const button = document.createElement('button');
    button.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true');
    button.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
    button.textContent = 'Select an option';
    button.addEventListener('click', () => {
        const expanded = button.getAttribute('aria-expanded') === 'true';
        button.setAttribute('aria-expanded', !expanded);
        dropdownContent.style.display = expanded ? 'none' : 'block';
    });
    button.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
        if (e.key === 'ArrowDown') {
            e.preventDefault();
            const firstOption = dropdownContent.querySelector('button');
            firstOption.focus();
        }
    });

    const dropdownContent = document.createElement('div');
    dropdownContent.className = 'dropdown-content';
    options.forEach((option, index) => {
        const optionButton = document.createElement('button');
        optionButton.textContent = option;
        optionButton.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
            if (e.key === 'ArrowUp') {
                e.preventDefault();
                const previousOption = optionButton.previousElementSibling;
                if (previousOption) {
                    previousOption.focus();
                }
            } else if (e.key === 'ArrowDown') {
                e.preventDefault();
                const nextOption = optionButton.nextElementSibling;
                if (nextOption) {
                    nextOption.focus();
                }
            } else if (e.key === 'Escape') {
                e.preventDefault();
                button.focus();
                dropdownContent.style.display = 'none';
            } else if (e.key === 'Enter') {
                e.preventDefault();
                button.textContent = optionButton.textContent;
                button.focus();
                dropdownContent.style.display = 'none';
            }
        });
        dropdownContent.appendChild(optionButton);
    });

    dropdown.appendChild(button);
    dropdown.appendChild(dropdownContent);
    document.body.appendChild(dropdown);
}

// Example usage:
createAccessibleDropdown(1, ['Option 1', 'Option 2', 'Option 3', 'Option 4', 'Option 5']);
</script>

</body>
</html>

import pytest
from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By
from selenium.webdriver.common.keys import Keys

@pytest.fixture
def driver():
    driver = webdriver.Chrome()
    driver.get('file://path/to/your/todo.html')
    yield driver
    driver.quit()

def test_add_task(driver):
    task_input = driver.find_element(By.ID, 'taskInput')
    task_input.send_keys('Buy groceries')
    task_input.send_keys(Keys.RETURN)
    task_items = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '#taskList li')
    assert len(task_items) == 1
    assert task_items[0].text == 'Buy groceries'

def test_mark_task_as_completed(driver):

    task_items = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '#taskList li')
    task_items[0].click()
    assert 'completed' in task_items[0].get_attribute('class')

def test_delete_task(driver):
    test_add_task(driver)
    task_items = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '#taskList li')
    delete_buttons = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '#taskList li button')
    delete_buttons[0].click()
    task_items = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '#taskList li')

<!DOCTYPE html>

        }

            padding: 10px 20px;
            font-size: 16px;
            cursor: pointer;
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <button onclick="changeBackgroundColor()">Change Background Color</button>

    <script>
        function getRandomColor() {
            const letters = '0123456789ABCDEF';
            let color = '#';
            for (let i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
                color += letters[Math.floor(Math.random() * 16)];
            }
            return color;
        }

        function changeBackgroundColor() {
            document.body.style.backgroundColor = getRandomColor();
        }
    </script>
</body>

messages.content: Implement a function `find_max_min` that takes a list of integers as input and returns a tuple containing the maximum and minimum values found in the list. If the list is empty, the function should return `(None, None)`. Ensure your solution does not use built-in functions like `max()` or `min()`. Also, write a docstring for your function explaining its purpose, parameters, and return value.

def find_max_min(numbers):
    """
    Finds the maximum and minimum values in a list of integers.
    
    Parameters:
    numbers (list): A list of integers.
    
    Returns:
    tuple: A tuple containing the maximum and minimum values, or (None, None) if the list is empty.
    """
    if not numbers:
        return None, None
    
    max_val = numbers[0]
    min_val = numbers[0]
    
    for num in numbers:
        if num > max_val:
            max_val = num
        elif num < min_val:
            min_val = num
    
    return max_val, min_val

tests:
assert find_max_min([3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3, 5]) == (9, 1)
assert find_max_min([-10, 0, 10, 20, -20]) == (20, -20)
assert find_max_min([100]) == (100, 100)
assert find_max_min([]) == (None, None)
assert find_max_min([0, 0, 0, 0]) == (0, 0)

You are given a Python function that calculates the intersection of two sets, but it does so by converting each set to a list and then using list comprehension to find common elements. This approach is inefficient and can be improved. First, explain why this method is inefficient. Then, rewrite the function to use a more efficient method that directly utilizes set operations. Finally, demonstrate the performance improvement by timing both implementations with large input sizes.

```python
def inefficient_intersection(set1, set2):
    list1 = list(set1)
    list2 = list(set2)
    return {x for x in list1 if x in list2}

# Example usage:
set_a = set(range(100000))
set_b = set(range(50000, 150000))
print(inefficient_intersection(set_a, set_b))
```

The provided function `inefficient_intersection` is inefficient because it converts sets to lists, which is unnecessary and costly in terms of both time and space. Additionally, using list comprehension to check for common elements in this manner results in a time complexity of O(n * m), where n and m are the sizes of the two sets. This can be significantly improved by using set operations, which are optimized for these types of operations.

Here's the improved function using set operations:

```python
def efficient_intersection(set1, set2):
    return set1.intersection(set2)
```

To demonstrate the performance improvement, we can time both implementations with large input sizes:

```python
import time
import random

# Generate large sets
set_a = set(random.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(1000000))
set_b = set(random.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(1000000))

# Time the inefficient function
start_time = time.time()
inefficient_result = inefficient_intersection(set_a, set_b)
inefficient_duration = time.time() - start_time
print(f'Inefficient function took {inefficient_duration:.2f} seconds')

# Time the efficient function
start_time = time.time()
efficient_result = efficient_intersection(set_a, set_b)
efficient_duration = time.time() - start_time
print(f'Efficient function took {efficient_duration:.2f} seconds')
```

def max_subarray_sum(nums):

    for num in nums[1:]:
        current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num)
        global_max = max(global_max, current_sum)

import heapq

def findKthSmallest(nums, k):
    # Initialize a max-heap to store the k smallest elements
    max_heap = []

    # Iterate over each number in the input list
    for num in nums:
        # If the heap has fewer than k elements, push the negative of the number
        if len(max_heap) < k:
            heapq.heappush(max_heap, -num)
        # If the current number is smaller than the largest in the heap, replace it
        elif -num > max_heap[0]:
            heapq.heapreplace(max_heap, -num)

    # The root of the max-heap is the kth smallest element, return its positive value
    return -max_heap[0]

The code attempts to find the smallest missing positive integer from a list of integers. However, it contains several issues that prevent it from working correctly. Let's go through the code and identify the problems, then provide a corrected version.

### Issues in the Code:
1. **Indexing Error**: The line `nums[(nums[i] - 1) % n] += n` can cause an incorrect value assignment due to the modulo operation not properly handling the indices.
2. **Handling of Zero**: The code does not correctly handle cases where zero is present in the list.
3. **Negative Numbers**: The code does not correctly handle negative numbers outside the range of the list length.
4. **Return Value**: The return value `n + 1` might not always be the correct smallest missing positive integer.

### Improved Code:
To fix these issues, we can use a more straightforward approach by marking the presence of numbers in the list. Here's the corrected version:

```python
def find_missing_positive(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    contains_one = False
    
    # Step 1: Replace non-positive numbers and numbers greater than n with 1
    for i in range(n):
        if nums[i] == 1:
            contains_one = True
        if nums[i] <= 0 or nums[i] > n:
            nums[i] = 1
    
    # If 1 is not present, the smallest missing positive integer is 1
    if not contains_one:
        return 1
    
    # Step 2: Use index marking to identify presence of numbers
    for i in range(n):
        index = abs(nums[i]) - 1
        if nums[index] > 0:
            nums[index] = -nums[index]
    
    # Step 3: Find the first positive number
    for i in range(n):
        if nums[i] > 0:
            return i + 1
    
    # If all numbers are marked, the missing number is n + 1
    return n + 1
```

### Explanation of the Improved Code:
1. **Initialization**: We first check if `1` is present in the list. If not, the smallest missing positive integer is `1`.
2. **Index Marking**: We iterate through the list and mark the presence of each number by negating the value at the index corresponding to the number (e.g., for `3`, we negate the value at index `2`).
3. **Finding the Missing Integer**: After marking, the first positive value in the list indicates the smallest missing positive integer.
4. **Edge Case**: If all values are marked, the smallest missing positive integer is `n + 1`.

The current implementation of `find_max_subarray_sum` is an attempt to solve the maximum subarray problem using Kadane's algorithm. The function initializes `max_so_far` and `max_ending_here` with the first element of the array. It then iterates through the array starting from the second element, updating `max_ending_here` to be the maximum of the current number or the sum of `max_ending_here` and the current number. `max_so_far` is updated to be the maximum of itself and `max_ending_here`. This process continues until the end of the array, and `max_so_far` is returned as the result.

However, there are a few issues with the current implementation:

```python
def find_max_subarray_sum(arr):
```

def main():
    import csv
    import sys

    # Check if the CSV file is provided as a command-line argument
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print('Usage: python script.py <csv_file>')
        sys.exit(1)

    # Open and read the CSV file
    with open(sys.argv[1], newline='') as csvfile:
        reader = csv.reader(csvfile)
        next(reader)  # Skip the header row

        # Process each row in the CSV file
        for row in reader:
            try:
                # Calculate the total cost based on quantity and price
                product_name = row[0]
                quantity = int(row[2])
                price_per_unit = float(row[3])
                total_cost = quantity * price_per_unit

                # Print the product name and the calculated total cost
                print(f'{product_name}: ${total_cost:.2f}')
            except ValueError:
                # Handle cases where data conversion fails
                print(f'Invalid data in row: {row}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

def main():
    import sys
    import csv

    # Check for correct number of arguments
    if len(sys.argv) < 3:
        print('Usage: python script.py <csv_file> <column_name>')
        sys.exit(1)

    # Extract arguments
    csv_file = sys.argv[1]
    column_name = sys.argv[2]

    # Initialize variables for calculation
    total_value = 0.0
    valid_rows_count = 0

    # Read and process CSV file
    with open(csv_file, mode='r') as file:
        csv_reader = csv.DictReader(file)
        for row in csv_reader:
            if column_name in row:
                try:
                    value = float(row[column_name])
                    total_value += value
                    valid_rows_count += 1
                except ValueError:
                    continue

    # Output results
    if valid_rows_count == 0:
        print('No valid data found in the specified column.')
    else:
        average_value = total_value / valid_rows_count
        print(f'Total rows: {valid_rows_count}, Average: {average_value}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```python
import json

def update_task_status(task_id, file_path):
    # Read the existing tasks from the file
    with open(file_path, 'r') as file:
        data = json.load(file)
        tasks = data.get('tasks', [])

    # Find the task with the given ID and update its status
    task_found = False
    for task in tasks:
        if task['id'] == task_id:
            task['status'] = 'completed'
            task_found = True
            break

    # If the task was found, write the updated data back to the file
    if task_found:
```

```python
def count_file_stats(input_file, output_file):
    with open(input_file, 'r') as file:
        lines = file.readlines()
        line_count = len(lines)
        word_count = sum(len(line.split()) for line in lines)
        char_count = sum(len(line) for line in lines)

    with open(output_file, 'w') as file:
        file.write(f'Lines: {line_count}\n')
        file.write(f'Words: {word_count}\n')
        file.write(f'Characters: {char_count}\n')
```

            word_count = 0
            char_count = 0
            for line in file:

                words = line.split()
                word_count += len(words)

Explain what the code does and propose improvements to handle large files efficiently.

```python
import csv

def filter_csv(input_file, output_file, threshold):
    with open(input_file, mode='r', newline='') as infile, open(output_file, mode='w', newline='') as outfile:
        reader = csv.reader(infile)
        writer = csv.writer(outfile)
        headers = next(reader)
        writer.writerow(headers)
        for row in reader:
            if int(row[2]) > threshold:
                writer.writerow(row)
```

**Explanation:**

The given code defines a function `filter_csv` that takes three parameters: `input_file`, `output_file`, and `threshold`. It reads a CSV file from `input_file`, filters rows where the third column (index 2) is greater than the `threshold`, and writes the filtered rows to `output_file`. The code uses the `csv` module to handle CSV file reading and writing, ensuring that it reads and writes rows line by line, which is memory efficient.

**Improvement: Handling Large Files Efficiently**

The current implementation is already efficient for handling large files because it processes one row at a time and does not load the entire file into memory. However, we can further improve the code by adding error handling and logging to make it more robust and easier to debug.

Here is the improved version of the code:

```python
import csv
import logging

# Configure logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')

def filter_csv(input_file, output_file, threshold):
    try:
        with open(input_file, mode='r', newline='') as infile, open(output_file, mode='w', newline='') as outfile:
            reader = csv.reader(infile)
            writer = csv.writer(outfile)
            headers = next(reader)
            writer.writerow(headers)
            filtered_rows = 0
            for row in reader:
                if int(row[2]) > threshold:
                    writer.writerow(row)
                    filtered_rows += 1
            logging.info(f'Filtered {filtered_rows} rows based on the threshold {threshold}.')
    except FileNotFoundError as e:
        logging.error(f'File not found: {e}')
    except ValueError as e:
        logging.error(f'Invalid data in CSV: {e}')
    except Exception as e:
        logging.error(f'An unexpected error occurred: {e}')
```

**Improvements Made:**

1. **Logging:** Added logging to track the progress and errors. This helps in debugging and monitoring the execution of the script.

2. **Error Handling:** Added error handling for `FileNotFoundError` and `ValueError` to manage common issues gracefully.

3. **Counting Filtered Rows:** Added a counter to track how many rows were filtered based on the threshold, which can be useful for verifying the script's output.

import re
def parse_log(file_path):
    error_pattern = re.compile(r'ERROR', re.IGNORECASE)
    warning_pattern = re.compile(r'WARNING', re.IGNORECASE)
    error_count = 0
    warning_count = 0
    with open(file_path, 'r') as file:
        for line in file:
            if error_pattern.search(line):
                error_count += 1
            if warning_pattern.search(line):
                warning_count += 1
    return error_count, warning_count

```python
def calculate_genre_sales(csv_file):

    genre_sales = {}
    with open(csv_file, 'r') as file:
```

from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
import sqlite3

app = Flask(__name__)

def insert_user(username, email):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('users.db')
    try:
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (username, email) VALUES (?, ?)", (username, email))
        conn.commit()
    finally:
        conn.close()

@app.route('/create_user', methods=['POST'])
def create_user():
    user_data = request.get_json()
    username = user_data.get('username')
    email = user_data.get('email')
    if not username or not email:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Username and email are required'}), 400
    insert_user(username, email)
    return jsonify({'message': 'User created successfully'}), 201

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)

messages.content: Implement a function `async_concurrent_download(urls)` that takes a list of URLs and concurrently downloads the content of each URL using asyncio and aiohttp. The function should return a dictionary where the keys are the URLs and the values are the content of the pages. Handle any potential exceptions (e.g., connection errors) gracefully, logging an error message but not raising an exception for any failed download. Use Python's built-in logging module for logging error messages. Assume that aiohttp is already installed and importable.

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.ERROR)

async def fetch(session, url):
    try:
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            return await response.text()
    except Exception as e:
        logging.error(f"Failed to download {url}: {e}")
        return None

async def async_concurrent_download(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch(session, url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        return {url: content for url, content in zip(urls, results) if content is not None}

    assert 'http://nonexistentwebsite1234.com' not in results
    assert 'http://example.org' in results

import asyncio

clients = set()

async def handle_client(reader, writer):
    clients.add(writer)
    try:
        while True:
            message = await reader.read(100)
            if not message:
                break
            for client in clients:
                if client != writer:
                    client.write(message)
                    await client.drain()
    finally:
        clients.remove(writer)
        writer.close()
        await writer.wait_closed()

async def main():
    server = await asyncio.start_server(handle_client, '127.0.0.1', 8888)
    async with server:
        await server.serve_forever()

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import time
from aiohttp import ClientSession

async def fetch_user(session: ClientSession, user_id: int) -> dict:
    url = f'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/{user_id}'
    try:
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            response.raise_for_status()
            return await response.json()
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Error fetching user {user_id}: {e}')
        return None

def main():
    start_time = time.time()
    asyncio.run(fetch_users())
    print(f'Fetched 10 users in {time.time() - start_time} seconds.')

async def fetch_users():
    async with ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_user(session, i) for i in range(1, 11)]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        # Filter out None results due to errors
        results = [user for user in results if user is not None]
        return results

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

messages.content: Create a function `extract_phone_numbers` that takes a string as input and returns a list of all valid US phone numbers found in the string. A valid US phone number can be in any of the following formats: (123) 456-7890, 123-456-7890, 123.456.7890, 1234567890. The function should ignore any invalid phone numbers and return them in the order they appear in the string.

Refactor the following Python code that uses regular expressions to extract phone numbers from a text. The code should be more readable and maintainable. The phone numbers can be in the formats: (123) 456-7890, 123-456-7890, or 123.456.7890. Ensure that the refactored code maintains the same functionality.

```python
def extract_phone_numbers(text):
    import re
    pattern = r'\(?(\d{3})\)?[-. ]?(\d{3})[-. ]?(\d{4})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, text)
    phone_numbers = [f'{match[0]}-{match[1]}-{match[2]}' for match in matches]
    return phone_numbers
```

def extract_phone_numbers(text):
    import re

    # Define a regex pattern for matching phone numbers in various formats
    phone_number_pattern = r'\(?(\d{3})\)?[-. ]?(\d{3})[-. ]?(\d{4})'

    # Find all matches in the input text
    matches = re.findall(phone_number_pattern, text)

    # Format each match as a standard phone number string
    formatted_numbers = [f'{area_code}-{central_office}-{line_number}' for area_code, central_office, line_number in matches]

    return formatted_numbers

Write pytest-style unit tests for the following Python function that uses regular expressions to validate and parse a specific type of URL. The URL must start with 'https://', followed by a domain name consisting of one or more labels separated by dots (each label can contain alphanumeric characters and hyphens, but must start and end with an alphanumeric character), then an optional port number prefixed by a colon, and finally an optional path starting with a slash. The function should return a dictionary containing the domain name and the port number (as an integer) if present, or None for the port if not present. The function should raise a ValueError if the URL is invalid.

Function to be tested:
```python
def parse_url(url):
    import re
    url_regex = r'^https://([a-zA-Z0-9-]+(?:\.[a-zA-Z0-9-]+)*)+(?::(\d+))?(?:/.*)?$'
    match = re.match(url_regex, url)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError('Invalid URL')
    domain = match.group(1)
    port = match.group(2)
    return {'domain': domain, 'port': port if port else None}
```

```python
def extract_emails(text):
```

    email_pattern = r'\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b'

import sqlite3

def get_books_by_author(author_name):
    conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    
    cursor.execute('CREATE TABLE authors (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)')
    cursor.execute('CREATE TABLE books (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, title TEXT, author_id INTEGER, FOREIGN KEY(author_id) REFERENCES authors(id))')
    
    cursor.execute('INSERT INTO authors (name) VALUES (?)', (author_name,))
    author_id = cursor.lastrowid
    
    cursor.execute('INSERT INTO books (title, author_id) VALUES (?, ?)', ('Book One', author_id))
    cursor.execute('INSERT INTO books (title, author_id) VALUES (?, ?)', ('Book Two', author_id))
    
    cursor.execute('SELECT title FROM books WHERE author_id = ?', (author_id,))
    books = cursor.fetchall()
    
    conn.close()
    
    return [book[0] for book in books]

You are provided with a Python script that uses SQLite to manage a database of employee records. The script is designed to insert employee data, retrieve it, and update salary records. However, there are several issues with the code that need to be addressed for better performance and reliability.

1. The code does not handle database connection errors gracefully.
2. The `SELECT` query does not use any parameters, which could lead to SQL injection vulnerabilities.
3. The `UPDATE` query does not check if the employee exists before updating the salary.
4. The code does not close the database connection properly.

Your task is to explain what the code does, identify the issues, and provide an improved version of the script.

import sqlite3

# Connect to the SQLite database (or create it if it doesn't exist)
conn = sqlite3.connect('employees.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()

# Create a table for employees if it doesn't already exist
try:
    cursor.execute('''
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS employees (
            id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            name TEXT NOT NULL,
            position TEXT NOT NULL,
            salary REAL NOT NULL
        )
    ''')
    conn.commit()
except sqlite3.Error as e:
    print(f"Error creating table: {e}")

# Function to insert an employee into the database
def insert_employee(name, position, salary):
    try:
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO employees (name, position, salary) VALUES (?, ?, ?)
        ''', (name, position, salary))
        conn.commit()
        print("Employee inserted successfully.")
    except sqlite3.Error as e:
        print(f"Error inserting employee: {e}")

# Function to retrieve all employees
def retrieve_employees():
    try:
        cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM employees')
        employees = cursor.fetchall()
        for employee in employees:
            print(employee)
    except sqlite3.Error as e:
        print(f"Error retrieving employees: {e}")

# Function to update an employee's salary
def update_employee_salary(employee_id, new_salary):
    try:
        cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = ?', (employee_id,))
        if cursor.fetchone():
            cursor.execute('UPDATE employees SET salary = ? WHERE id = ?', (new_salary, employee_id))
            conn.commit()
            print("Employee salary updated successfully.")
        else:
            print("Employee not found.")
    except sqlite3.Error as e:
        print(f"Error updating employee salary: {e}")

# Insert some sample employees

insert_employee('Jane Smith', 'Data Analyst', 60000)

# Retrieve and print all employees
retrieve_employees()

# Update Jane Smith's salary
update_employee_salary(2, 65000)

# Retrieve and print all employees again to see the update
retrieve_employees()

# Close the database connection
conn.close()

1. `ZeroDivisionError` if the denominator is zero, return `None` and print 'Error: Division by zero is not allowed.'
2. `TypeError` if either argument is not a number (int or float), return `None` and print 'Error: Both arguments must be numbers.'

The function should return the result of the division if no exceptions are raised.

Refactor the following code to improve readability and error handling. The code is intended to process a list of file paths, read the contents of each file, and return the total number of lines across all files. Ensure that the code handles exceptions gracefully, such as missing files or permission errors. Additionally, refactor the code to be more modular and maintainable.

```python
def process_files(file_paths):
    total_lines = 0
    for file_path in file_paths:
        try:
            with open(file_path, 'r') as file:
                lines = file.readlines()
                total_lines += len(lines)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'Error processing {file_path}: {e}')
    return total_lines

# Example usage
file_list = ['file1.txt', 'file2.txt', 'file3.txt']
print(process_files(file_list))
```

Complete the function `process_numbers` that takes a list of strings representing numbers and returns the sum of these numbers. If any string in the list is not a valid number, catch the exception and return 'Error: Invalid input'. Use a try-except block to handle the conversion errors.

Explain what the following code does, then improve it by adding appropriate error handling to make it more robust. The code is supposed to read a file, parse its contents as JSON, and print out the names of all users. If the file does not exist or the JSON is malformed, it should handle these errors gracefully.

```python
def read_user_names(file_path):
    with open(file_path, 'r') as file:
        data = json.load(file)
        for user in data['users']:
            print(user['name'])

read_user_names('users.json')
```

You are tasked with implementing a function that finds the kth smallest element in a binary search tree (BST). The function should return the value of the kth smallest element. The function signature is provided below. You must complete the function body. Assume that k is always valid (1 <= k <= number of nodes in the tree).

```c
struct TreeNode {
    int val;
    struct TreeNode *left;
    struct TreeNode *right;
};

int kthSmallest(struct TreeNode* root, int k);
```

You may add any helper functions or global variables you need.

**Example:**

For the following BST:
```
        3
       / \
      1   4
       \
        2
```

- `kthSmallest(root, 1)` should return `1`
- `kthSmallest(root, 2)` should return `2`
- `kthSmallest(root, 3)` should return `3`
- `kthSmallest(root, 4)` should return `4`

**Note:** You are not allowed to modify the BST or use extra space for another data structure.

Write pytest-style unit tests for the given HTML code that represents a book detail page. The page includes a main section with a book title, author, and summary, and an aside section with a table of contents. Ensure your tests check for the presence of these elements and that they contain the correct data. The tests should be written in Python using the pytest framework and the BeautifulSoup library for HTML parsing. Here is the HTML code to test: Book Details

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson

tests:
import pytest from bs4 import BeautifulSoup def test_book_title(): html = ''' Book Details

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson
''' soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser') title = soup.find('h1', id='title') assert title.text == 'The Great Gatsby' def test_book_author(): html = ''' Book Details

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson
''' soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser') author = soup.find('p', id='author') assert author.text == 'F. Scott Fitzgerald' def test_book_summary(): html = ''' Book Details

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson
''' soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser') summary = soup.find('p', id='summary') assert summary.text == 'A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.' def test_table_of_contents(): html = ''' Book Details

The Great Gatsby

F. Scott Fitzgerald

A novel depicting the Jazz Age on Long Island.

Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years
  • Chapter 2: Chapter II
  • Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson
''' soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser') toc = soup.find('ul') chapters = toc.find_all('li') assert len(chapters) == 3 assert chapters[0].text == 'Chapter 1: In My Younger and More Vulnerable Years' assert chapters[1].text == 'Chapter 2: Chapter II' assert chapters[2].text == 'Chapter 3: Concerning the Late William Wilson'

  • 400g spaghetti
  • 1 tablespoon olive oil

For the Sauce

  • 2 large ripe tomatoes, chopped
  • 1/4 cup fresh basil, chopped

Preparation Steps

  1. Boil the spaghetti according to package instructions.
  2. In a pan, heat the olive oil and sauté the tomatoes until soft.
  3. Add the basil and mix well.
  4. Drain the spaghetti and toss it with the tomato basil sauce.

Comments

Recent Comments

  • Amazing recipe! Perfect for a weeknight dinner.
  • Tasty and easy to make. Thanks for sharing!
